subject: english
chapter_title: How I Taught My Grandmother to Read (How I Taught My Grandmother to Read)

main_sections.title: How I Taught My Grandmother to Read
main_sections.prose_summary:
The story is narrated by a young girl of twelve who spends time in a village in north Karnataka with her grandparents. Set in an era of poor transport and delayed newspapers, the household eagerly awaits the weekly Karmaveera magazine, which carries a serialised Kannada novel, Kashi Yatre, by the beloved writer Triveni. The novel tells of an old woman who sacrifices her life savings to fund an orphan girl's wedding rather than fulfil her own dream of visiting Kashi — a choice that moves readers deeply. The narrator's grandmother, Krishtakka (called Avva, meaning 'mother' in north Karnataka Kannada), is illiterate and depends entirely on the narrator to read each episode aloud. She listens with total concentration and discusses the story with friends at the temple courtyard, deeply identifying with the protagonist since she, too, never went to Kashi.

When the narrator goes to a cousin's wedding in a neighbouring village and overstays for a week, she returns to find her grandmother in tears — unprecedented for a woman who has faced life's hardships stoically. In a tender midnight conversation on the open terrace, Krishtakka reveals the cause: the magazine arrived while the narrator was away, and she could not read the next episode. She felt helpless and ashamed to ask neighbours for help, and the experience exposed her painful dependence despite the family's financial comfort.

Krishtakka declares a bold resolution: she will learn the Kannada alphabet, use Saraswati Puja day during Dassara as her deadline, and read a novel on her own. The narrator initially laughs at the idea of her grey-haired, wrinkled sixty-two-year-old grandmother going back to basics, but Krishtakka responds with quiet dignity — 'For a good cause if you are determined, you can overcome any obstacle. For learning there is no age bar.' Tuition begins the next morning. Krishtakka proves to be a diligent student: she reads, repeats, writes, and recites. By the Dassara festival she meets her deadline. In a deeply symbolic gesture, the grandmother touches the narrator's feet — reversing the traditional elder–younger hierarchy — explaining that she is honouring not her granddaughter but her teacher. The narrator gives her gift: a copy of Kashi Yatre, now published as a novel. Krishtakka reads the title and the publisher's name immediately, confirming she has 'passed with flying colours'. The story, authored by Sudha Murty, celebrates literacy, determination, and the transformative power of intergenerational love.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Reflect and Respond + Reading for Meaning + Check Your Understanding + Critical Reflection

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the given word web. [Central question: Why is it important to learn how to read and write?]
Read the questions given below and share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: Which language(s) do your grandparents or elderly relatives speak?
How do they spend their time? How do you spend time with them?
What is your favourite experience with them?
What is something that the elderly in your family cannot do easily but enjoy watching you do?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following passage. Match the highlighted words with their meanings given in the box below. [Words: protagonist, debate, episode, community, concentration, eagerly, convincing, guided. Meanings: excitedly, believable, people living in one particular area, discussion, a part of a story, focus, main character, directed]

question_bank.stem: protagonist
debate
episode
community
concentration
eagerly
convincing
guided

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the cause and effect table given below based on Part I of the story. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: Cause for effect: The morning papers arrived late in the day and weekly magazines would come one day late.
Effect of: The grandmother, Krishtakka, never went to school.
Cause for effect: She was deeply interested in the story and discussed it with her friends.
Effect of: The narrator went to a wedding for a week.
Cause for effect (v): The grandmother never went to school.
Effect of: The grandmother regretted not going to school.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do you think the narrator expected to see her grandmother in tears when she returned to the village? If yes, why? If no, why not?
How might the narrator help her grandmother to fulfil her desire to learn to read and write?
State whether the following sentences are true or false. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: The grandmother wanted to learn the Kannada alphabet to gain independence.
The grandmother asked someone in the village to read Kashi Yatre to her while the narrator was away.
The narrator was the grandmother's first teacher and taught her how to read Kannada.
The grandmother believed that there was no age limit for learning.
The grandmother touched the narrator's feet as a mark of respect for her as a teacher.
The narrator was disappointed with the progress her grandmother made in learning to read.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. [Extract 1: 'When I came back to my village, I saw my grandmother in tears...' to '...Her affectionate hands touched my forehead.']

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence with the appropriate option. The phrase 'never seen her cry in the most difficult situations' tells us that the grandmother was ____.

Complete the following with the correct option from those given in brackets. Grandmother did not reply when the narrator asked if she was alright because she might have been too ____ (emotional/tired) to respond.
Identify the clue from the extract that indicates a rural setting with traditional customs.
Which lines of the extract establish a tender atmosphere?
Which of the following aspect is NOT emphasised in the given extract?
options: the emotional turmoil of the grandmother
the affectionate bond between the narrator and her grandmother
the grandmother's regret over her lack of education
the narrator's concern for her grandmother

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. [Extract 2: 'I have decided I want to learn the Kannada alphabet...' to '...Childishly I made fun of the old lady. But she just smiled.']

question_bank.stem: What does the grandmother's statement, 'I want to be independent,' reveal about her character?
options: She wants to prove her intelligence to others.
She feels pressured by society to learn.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blank with the appropriate option from those given in brackets. The grandmother's determination shows that learning has no ____. (age limit/gender bias/cultural barriers)
Complete the following with a suitable reason. The narrator laughs at her grandmother's decision to learn the alphabet at the age of sixty-two because ____.
List any two qualities displayed by the grandmother.
How can we say that the narrator is making assumptions about her grandmother?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Why do you think the grandmother felt embarrassed to ask someone else to read to her while the narrator was away?
Why does the narrator initially laugh at her grandmother's determination to learn at the age of sixty-two?
What significance does the story of Kashi Yatre have in both the grandmother's life and the story?
What does the grandmother's desire to learn the Kannada alphabet reflect about her?
What lessons can we infer from the grandmother's action of touching the narrator's feet?
What does the following line tell us about the broader theme of the story? 'For a good cause if you are determined, you can overcome any obstacle.'
How effectively does the story highlight the value of education in supporting personal independence?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The expression 'hide and seek' is used in the text. This is called a binomial. Match the binomials in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. You may refer to a dictionary. [Column 1: sink or swim, on and off, mix and match, all or nothing, part and parcel, pick and choose, sooner or later, leaps and bounds] Now, use any five of the above binomials in sentences of your own.

question_bank.stem: sink or swim
on and off
mix and match
all or nothing
part and parcel
pick and choose
sooner or later
leaps and bounds

These words are made by adding suitable prefixes ('un', 'ir', and 'in') to give an opposite or negative meaning. Now, make words by adding the suitable prefixes given in the box [un, im, dis, in, mis, extra] to the words in Column 1. tasks_verbatim.task_text: Identify any five words with prefixes from the story and make sentences using each.

In the sentence, 'I knew, then, that my student had passed with flying colours', the phrase 'passed with flying colours' is an idiom. Match the idioms related to 'learning' given in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. [Idioms: to hit the books, to draw a blank, to learn the ropes, to rack one's brain, to learn by heart, burn the midnight oil] Now, use these idioms in sentences of your own.

question_bank.stem: to hit the books
to draw a blank
to learn the ropes
to rack one's brain
to learn by heart
burn the midnight oil

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following sentences from the text and underline the verbs. [1. Secretly, I bought Kashi Yatre which had been published as a novel by that time. 2. I knew, then, that my student had passed with flying colours.] [Grammar box: past perfect tense vs simple past.] Fill in the blanks with simple past and past perfect tense form of the verbs given in brackets.

question_bank.stem: A. When the delegates __ (arrive) at the conference, the keynote speaker __ (already begin) the session.
B. After the students __ (learn) how to identify fake news online, they __ (start) verifying information before sharing it.
C. Before Kiran __ (start) using digital payment platforms, she __ (ensure) her understanding of online fraud prevention.
D. By the time Varun __ (recognise) the importance of budgeting, he __ (exhaust) most of his savings.
E. When Raghu __ (log in) to the cybersecurity webinar, the instructor __ (already discuss) the importance of strong passwords.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks with the correct form of verbs given in brackets. [Paragraph: Last year, my parents and I A. __ (take) a financial planning course. When we B. __ (review) our expenses, we realised we C. __ (spend) too much on unnecessary purchases. After my parents D. __ (discuss) ways to save, I E. __ (open) a savings account. By the time we F. __ (set) our budget, the course G. __ (already introduce) investment strategies. We H. __ (hurry) to take notes, but many participants I. __ (complete) their financial plans. Despite that, we J. __ (enjoy) learning how to manage money wisely.]

question_bank.stem: A (take)
B (review)
C (spend)
D (discuss)
E (open)
F (set)
G (already introduce)
H (hurry)
I (complete)
J (enjoy)

listening.transcript_text: Good morning, everyone. Today, I would like to discuss an essential skill in our daily lives — digital literacy. Earlier, literacy meant just reading and writing. Now, it includes the ability to use digital tools effectively and responsibly. Digital literacy means accessing, creating, and sharing information using technology. It's not just about using gadgets but also understanding digital platforms, identifying reliable sources, and protecting personal data. Digital literacy is important for three reasons: first, it empowers individuals — students learn to research critically, professionals handle digital transactions, and senior citizens stay connected; second, it promotes safe internet use by helping us recognise misinformation, scams, and cyber threats; and third, it prepares us for a digital future where skills in online services, job applications, and governance are essential. The Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (DISHA) or National Digital Literacy Mission (NDLM) Scheme has been formulated to impart IT training to people across the country.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a speaker talk about digital literacy. As you listen, complete the following sentences with one to three exact words that you hear. (Transcript for teacher on page 259)

question_bank.stem: Literacy these days includes the ability to use ____________.
Digital literacy is also about protecting ____________.
Digital literacy enables the elderly to ______________.
Digital literacy encourages using the internet safely by helping us recognise ______________ and ______________.
The National Digital Literacy Mission helps people across the country by imparting ______________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Turncoat is a type of solo debate where the speaker argues for and against a topic, switching sides after a certain period of time. Choose your topic and speak 'for' and 'against' for not more than one minute each. Topic 1: It is important to learn a new language apart from your mother tongue. Topic 2: Learning can happen only when you are young. Begin with speaking 'for' for one minute, then switch and speak 'against' for one minute. [Sentence prompts provided for both sides.]

question_bank.stem: Topic 1: It is important to learn a new language apart from your mother tongue. — Speak FOR then AGAINST.
Topic 2: Learning can happen only when you are young. — Speak FOR then AGAINST.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: As a socially responsible citizen, you strongly believe that students can contribute significantly in promoting adult literacy. Write a letter to the Editor of a local newspaper emphasising the importance of student participation in adult literacy camps conducted by various organisations. Discuss the benefits of such initiatives for both students and society. Suggest effective ways to spread awareness and encourage more students to volunteer for this noble cause. [Format guidance: POINTS TO REMEMBER — Name/designation/address, Date, Subject (4–5 words), Salutation, 3-paragraph body, Complimentary close: Yours truly. Language: formal, clear, specific.]

India has 22 major languages according to the Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution. Each language has its own unique identity. Talk to your classmates and teacher and complete the given table with words from any five Indian languages. [Table rows: Grandmother, Grandfather, Mother, Father, Sister, Brother; Column 1 filled: Kannada — Avva for Grandmother]

question_bank.stem: Fill in kinship terms (Grandmother, Grandfather, Mother, Father, Sister, Brother) in five Indian languages of your choice.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do you know? [Four informational passages: (1) National Literacy Mission Authority (NLMA) and Directorate of Adult Education — link: https://dsel.education.gov.in/adult_education; (2) Financial literacy and the National Centre for Financial Education (NCFE); (3) The Early Literacy Project (ELP) targeting out-of-school and marginalised children; (4) Cultural literacy and the Centre for Cultural Resources and Training (CCRT). Students engage with these texts as part of broader literacy awareness.]

question_bank.stem: Find more information on the National Literacy Mission Authority using https://dsel.education.gov.in/adult_education
Read about financial literacy and the NCFE's role in integrating financial education.
Read about the Early Literacy Project (ELP) and its target community.
Read about cultural literacy and the CCRT's programmes.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Talk to your grandparents and find out which book or magazine they enjoy reading. Buy a copy of it and present it to them as a gift.
